dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for allergic reaction.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My face feels like it's burning, and it's been like this for maybe 30 minutes.
He's in the wheelchair. I don't know what caused it, but his face is very flushed.
Does he have any pain with this?
No, no pain-just a weird burning feeling on my face and some itching around both cheeks.
Can you tell me your name?
Yes, my name is Michael.
What city are we in?
Richmond. Richmond, Virginia.
What's today's date?
I think it's the 29th.
What happened today?
I don't know. My face feels strange, I think I had a reaction.
Can you speak clearly? Are you having any trouble breathing right now?
No, I'm fine now. Just my face.
Partner, let me know the BP and pulse.
BP 100 over 64, and pulse is 59.
Respirations are 20 and unlabored.
How about your breathing, Michael? Any trouble?
No. I felt some shortness of breath earlier, but it's better now.
Good. Your oxygen saturation looks good too, at 98% right now.
Skin is warm and dry. Do you have any numbness, tingling, or swelling anywhere else?
Not really, just my face. It's flushed and feels like it's burning and itching slightly too.
SpO2 still 98% and skin color is normal with no cyanosis observed.
Your face does look flushed. Is there any rash, hives, or swelling elsewhere?
No obvious hives. It just feels hot and itchy in some spots.
Pupils look equal and reactive. Can you move your eyes side to side and up and down for me?
Yeah, I can.
Good. Your trachea looks midline and you don't have swollen neck veins or visible masses.
Upper extremities have movement and sensation. Lower body is paralyzed, no deformities or swelling noted.
So, Michael, when exactly did the burning begin?
Maybe 30 minutes ago. I don't know what I touched. It came on gradually after eating something, but the meal was normal per recent history.
Have you had any recent changes in medications or new foods besides pizza we heard about earlier?
No, I had my usual meds and some pizza earlier. I take Baclofen in the morning-always the same dose. No new experimental substances, known or not, this morning.
That's all he had. No unusual activity or animals.
EKG is sinus bradycardia currently. Rhythm consistent with baseline measurements thus far.
Bradycardia noted, but rhythm is stable. Any chest discomfort or palpitations with it?
No discomfort, just the burning in my face again.
What medications are you taking regularly?
I take Baclofen, Zyrtec, Zantac, duloxetine, Cymbalta, sennosides-some things hard to say the full names to.
Including Cetirizine, Docusate/Senna, Trospium, Sennosides, and some USP preparations.
Any known allergies or things you can't take, Michael?
I'm allergic to Lyrica.
What other conditions have affected or likely affect your health, aside from the ongoing injury?
I have anxiety, mainly-but other than that, no major illnesses-no inherited or major condition either, I think.
When was your last substantial meal or drink before the onset occurred?
Pizza and water, maybe 2 hours ago.
Let's check your glucose now, just to confirm.
Glucose is listed as 114 mg/dL-nothing to worry about.
I'm going to place you on chest monitoring for baseline-make sure our rhythm assessment is complete.
I just set up the 4-lead ECG monitoring per protocol. Rhythm read as sinus bradycardia.
Bradycardia noted, but rhythm is stable. Any chest discomfort or pain with it?
No, just the burning in my face again.
I'd like to give some Benadryl to help with the allergic reaction. Michael, is that okay with you?
IV access established in the left arm. Providing 50 mg of Benadryl now.
BP is now 107 over 70. Pulse has increased to 72 beats per minute compared to pre-assessment.
SpO2 up to 100% now, with steady vitals and no new issues to report.
AOX4 noted with no change in consciousness.
GCS remains at 15-alert and oriented in all four domains.
Glucose reading still zero, likely due to interference.
BP is 108 over 72 now, with no significant irregularities.
Vitals are stable, no distress observed.
Based on vitals and physical state, we'll follow Allergic Reaction protocol-no signs of systemic distress observed.
I'm calling McGuire Hospital to alert the ER. They're expecting us now.
Let me move you from the wheelchair now on a semi-fowler's-help with positioning you safely for travel.
You'll be moved from the stretcher to the ER bed with secure positioning.
RN, our patient is a 28-year-old male presenting with an allergic reaction as face burning and flushing, with normal vitals recorded.
Allergies to Lyrica and 50 mg IV Benadryl given post-IV placement. Transport vitals are stable.
No distress during transport, and vitals maintained within normal limits.
Reassessing your medical history-still on stable medications with no new events?
Same meds, same day, no episodes since EMS arrived. I still feel okay, just the burning.
IV still patent, no infiltration noted.
Your chest is stable, breath sounds are clear. Heart sounds are regular.
Your stomach seems normal-no pain or masses.
No signs of bleeding or changes in color or frequency reported.
Ears normal, no draining. No obstruction or signs of infection. Gums clean.
Your pupils continue to react strongly. Tell me what you had for breakfast.
It was my usual routine-Baclofen, Zyrtec, maybe Zantac for that burning sensation.
Reviewing pain-none reported at any time.
Pulse is now 62. Still within normal range.
EKG's still stable-Sinus rhythm with no fluctuations.
Sticking with established allergic reaction treatment strategy.
Hospital confirmed our arrival will be greeted. They should be able to manage it.
Final secure confirmation. Patient resting in semi-fowler's position-set for transport.
Recorded vitals over 30-minute baseline before treatment-confirmed no pain reported.
No deviations in the pattern since medication administered. Vitals remain normal.
Routine case of allergic response. Benadryl administered per protocol.
Safe transport to the hospital.
Summary given to Sayra Cardona, RN. ICU not needed.
Delay for turnover. EMS and hospital communication reaffirmed.
Case closed-documented per established protocol and evaluation.
All set for transfer. Safe arrival expected.
Additional documentation updated with confirmation via cell. Patient reported stable.
All actions consistent with protocol. No further evaluation required.
Excellent cooperation from the patient. All equipment secure.
